title: Silicate cosmic dust grain collisions in the interstellar medium: A molecular dynamics study
authors: C. J. Esmerian, S. R. Hashemi, W. M. C. Sameera, W. Vlemmings, S. Andersson, T. J. L. C. Bakx, K. K. Knudsen, S. Aalto, G. Nyman
abstract: (abridged) We aim to predict the most important parameters for grain-grain collision outcomes for models of interstellar grain population evolution on astrophysical scales: the threshold velocity above which colliding grains shatter, the threshold for vaporization, and resulting distributions of grain sizes. We use molecular dynamics simulations which evolve the dynamics of each atom in a dust grain to explore the outcomes of collisions between silicate grains of radii $a \in [5,50]~Å$ at velocities $0.1-20$ km/s. We run simulations of grains with two materials: amorphous SiO$_2$ and an amorphous silicate of composition suggested by Draine \& Hensley (2021). With these simulations, we quantify the collision velocity dependence of shattered and vaporized mass fractions, and the resulting size distributions of shattering products. We find grain shattering thresholds are $\sim$6 km/s for both amorphous SiO$_2$ and astrodust material, which is a factor of $\sim$2 higher than the canonical value for silicates of 2.7 km/s from Jones et al. (1996). This discrepancy is mostly alleviated by correcting an error in the expression for these velocity thresholds derived in Tielens et al. (1994). We find that the size distributions of shattered products are generally not consistent with the power law distributions predicted by this previous model. We also find that their expression fails to predict the fraction of shattered or vaporized material observed in our numerical simulations. The model of Hirashita \& Kobayashi (2013) for the same quantities similarly fails to match the simulations. We provide updated shattering velocity thresholds for standard candidate grain materials to the astrophysics community. Broadly, our updated threshold velocities that astrophysical dust grains may be more robust to shattering in the interstellar medium than previously assumed.
summary: This paper uses molecular dynamics to show that interstellar silicate dust grains are more resistant to shattering during collisions than previously thought. By correcting an algebraic error in long-standing models, the authors provide updated velocity thresholds that suggest dust populations are more robust in the interstellar medium.
main_problem: Determining the physical outcomes (shattering, vaporization, or coagulation) of high-velocity collisions between nanoscale silicate dust grains in the interstellar medium and correcting errors in existing analytical threshold models.
main_result: The study finds that silicate grains are more resilient to shattering than previously assumed, with updated thresholds around 6 km/s compared to the canonical 2.7 km/s, and identifies that previous models fail to accurately predict product size distributions.
method: The authors use atomistic Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations with ReaxFF and GFN1-xTB force fields, alongside a re-derivation of shock jump conditions to correct algebraic errors in classical analytical frameworks.
model_or_system: The system consists of collisions between nanoscale silicate grains (amorphous SiO2 and 'astrodust' Mg-Fe-Si-O) with radii between 5 and 50 Angstroms at velocities of 0.1 to 20 km/s.
key_observables: Shattering and vaporization threshold velocities, mass fractions of shattered/vaporized material, and grain size distributions of the resulting products.
important_parameters: Collision velocity (0.1-20 km/s), grain radii (5-50 A), material composition (SiO2 and ADSil), and impact parameter (set to head-on).
main_assumptions: The simulations focus on the lowest decade of ISM grain sizes and neglect nuclear quantum effects and nonadiabatic excited-state processes due to computational costs.
figures_summary: Figures show the initial grain structures, mass fractions of disrupted/vaporized grains as a function of velocity, the evolution of grain shape and radius, and the cumulative size distributions of shattered products compared to power-law models.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of grain processing, describes the MD simulation setup and grain construction, presents results on mass fractions and size distributions, provides a mathematical correction to previous analytical shock models in an appendix, and concludes with the implications for ISM evolution.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Silica SiO2 structure candidates relaxed at 10 K. The structures are obtained using the procedure detailed in Section 2.2.1.
Fig. 2: Astrodust structures optimized using the GFN1-xTB method. The structures are obtained using the procedure detailed in Section 2.2.2.

Fig. 3: Collision outcomes for SiO2 grains of the same size as a function of impact velocity. Each panel shows the mass fractions, as a fraction of the total combined mass of initial particles, of the 3 main collision outcomes: the largest remaining grain (defined by mass, blue), shattered products smaller than the largest grain but larger than the vaporization limit (red), and the vaporized fraction of collision products smaller than our minimum grain size of 4.5Å (gray). Initial grain sizes are indicated by a1 and a2. The top left panel shows results for the S4–S4 simulations, top right for S3–S3, bottom left for S2–S2, and bottom right for S1–S1. White regions indicate velocities outside...
Fig. 4: Same as Figure 3 but for SiO2 grains with different sizes. The top panel shows results for the S2–S3 simulations, the bot- tom for S1–S3.
Fig. 5: Same as Figure 3 but for astrodust silicate (ADSil) grains of the same size. The top panel shows results for the A2–A2 simulations, the bottom for A1–A1.
